entry.title: Re: Supplier Down payment adjustments and tracking
entry.content:
Hi Vagula,

To address your requirement, we need to split it into two parts: tracking the purchasing side and tracking the subsequent sale to the customer.

1. Tracking the Purchasing Side (PO to Supplier Invoice) Yes, there is an app designed specifically for this phase. You can use the Monitor Purchase Order Down Payments app (App ID F2877). This app provides a comprehensive overview of the PO item, the associated down payment requests, the cleared down payments, and the final supplier invoices.

However, to make this work, the end-to-end traceability must not be broken during the bank loan posting. If your finance team processes the advance payment purely in Finance (FI) without a Purchase Order (e.g., creating it manually in the Manage Supplier Down Payment Requests app or posting it directly from a bank statement), the linkage to the logistics process is permanently lost. An FI-only posting records the supplier and the financial amount, but it lacks the material number, plant, and quantity. Without this data, tracking the product later is impossible.

To maintain full traceability, you must strictly follow the PO-based down payment process:

  • Maintain the down payment parameters at the header/item level using the Manage Purchase Orders or Create Purchase Order - Advanced app. Please note: Simply adding the expected down payment to the PO does not automatically create the financial document needed for payment. It merely acts as a commercial agreement.

  • You must actively generate the down payment request using the Monitor Purchase Order Down Payments app.

  • This Down Payment Request is posted with a Special G/L indicator F. It is created as a Noted Item, meaning it is a single-sided entry that does not immediately update the General Ledger balances. It only appears on the supplier balance to be picked up by the payment process.

  • Once the actual payment is made (or cleared), it converts to a regular Special G/L item (e.g., indicator 'A'), updating the G/L.

  • Finally, clear the down payment upon the receipt of the actual supplier invoice.

How does this work with a Bank Loan (Third-Party Payment)? Since the bank pays the supplier directly (and not from your standard house bank account via the Manage Automatic Payments app), the clearing of that Noted Item requires a manual FI step. Your finance team will need to use the Clear Outgoing Payments app (App ID F1367) or Post with Clearing to manually clear the open Down Payment Request against the bank loan liability account. This creates the actual Down Payment document linked to the PO while recognizing the debt to the bank.

You can review the standard process in the SAP Help documentation: Down Payment Processing or here Monitor Purchase Order Down Payments 

2. Tracing the Flow to the End Customer There is no single out-of-the-box app that acts as a standalone report tracing a supplier down payment all the way to a customer invoice. In standard inventory management (Make-to-Stock), Supply and Demand are decoupled, meaning once the goods enter your general stock, the system does not link a specific supplier invoice to a specific customer invoice.

However, full visual traceability is natively available via the "Process Flow" (Document Flow) feature if you use specific account-assigned logistics scenarios:

  • Third-Party Processing (Scope Items BDK / BD3): The Sales Order automatically generates a Purchase Requisition, which becomes the PO.

  • Make-to-Order (Scope Item BJE) or Account Assigned POs: The PO is directly pegged to the Customer Sales Order.

If you use these scenarios, you can open the Manage Sales Orders - Version 2 app, navigate to the specific Sales Order, and click on the Process Flow tab. The system will visually map the entire chain.

In case you have some specific process in place, please describe it in detail in the comment so I can actually react to your specific business scenario.

Best regards,

Michal

entry.title: Re: Business Objects Enterprise 4.3 update from SP04 patc...
entry.content:
Hi Andreas,

Based on what you've described, the symptoms strongly point to file locking by another process rather than a corrupted upgrade package. The fact that errors decreased between SP05 P4 and SP05 P5 attempts (but didn't disappear) and that the system actually works after the "failed" upgrade reinforces this.

Here are the most likely causes and what I'd suggest checking, roughly in order of probability:

1. Microsoft Defender is still scanning despite exclusions

Adding folders to Defender's exclusion list via the GUI sometimes doesn't apply to all engines (e.g., real-time protection, controlled folder access, ASR rules, cloud-delivered protection, tamper protection). On Windows Server 2022, Defender is particularly aggressive.

What to try:

  • Verify exclusions with PowerShell: Get-MpPreference | Select-Object ExclusionPath, ExclusionProcess, ExclusionExtension
  • Add process-level exclusions, not just paths:
    • setup.exe, BusinessObjectsServer.exe, boe_setup.exe, msiexec.exe, cscript.exe, java.exe, wdeploy*.exe
  • Also exclude:
    • D:\SAP BusinessObjects\ (whole tree)
    • D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\tmp\
    • D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\InstallCache\
    • The temp folder used by the installer (%TEMP%, %LOCALAPPDATA%\Temp, C:\Windows\Temp)
    • The folder containing the upgrade media
  • Temporarily disable real-time protection (Set-MpPreference -DisableRealtimeMonitoring $true) for the duration of the upgrade. This is the most reliable test — if it succeeds, Defender is the culprit.
  • Check if Controlled Folder Access or ASR rules are enabled (Get-MpPreference | fl *Asr*, *Controlled*) and disable them temporarily.

2. Residual CrowdStrike components

Even after uninstalling CrowdStrike Falcon, the kernel-mode sensor driver (CSAgent.sys, csagent) can sometimes remain loaded until reboot, or be only partially uninstalled if uninstall protection was enabled.

What to check:

Get-Service csagent -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
sc.exe query csagent
Get-ChildItem "C:\Windows\System32\drivers\CSAgent.sys" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue

If anything is found, ensure a complete removal (you may need the maintenance token from CrowdStrike) and reboot before re-attempting the upgrade.

3. Windows Search / Indexing service

The Windows Search indexer can lock files in subfolders. Exclude D:\SAP BusinessObjects\ from indexing, or stop the Windows Search service during the upgrade:

Stop-Service WSearch

4. VSS / Backup agents

If the VM has a backup agent (Veeam, Commvault, Azure Backup, etc.) or is part of a VMware/Hyper-V scheduled snapshot, VSS writers can briefly lock files. Check if a backup window overlapped your upgrade window. Disable the backup job during the upgrade.

5. The \\?\ long-path prefix in the error

The error Access to file denied: \\?\D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\tmp\... is interesting because the \\?\ prefix means the installer is using long-path APIs. Combined with FileAccessDeniedException from Poco, this is almost always another process holding a handle, not a real ACL issue.

You can confirm with Process Monitor (Sysinternals) during the next attempt:

  • Filter on Path containing InstallData\tmp and Result = ACCESS DENIED or SHARING VIOLATION
  • The "Process Name" column will tell you exactly which process is locking the file (Defender's MsMpEng.exe, an EDR agent, the indexer, a backup agent, etc.)

This is the single most useful diagnostic step I would recommend — it will give you a definitive answer rather than guessing.

6. Other suggestions

  • Disconnect the VM from the network during the upgrade (after taking your snapshot). This prevents Defender cloud-delivered protection and any centrally pushed EDR policy from re-activating mid-install.
  • Reboot immediately before running the upgrade so no stale handles exist.
  • Stop the SIA service manually before launching setup (the installer should do this, but doing it explicitly avoids race conditions).
  • Make sure no Tomcat/Java processes are still running after the SIA stops (Get-Process java, tomcat*).
  • Check Windows Event Viewer (Application + Defender Operational log) at the timestamps in ErrorsAndWarnings.log — Defender often logs the exact file it quarantined or blocked.

About the "successful failure"

What you observed (the system works fine after the upgrade despite the error message) is consistent with the errors being on InstallCache copy operations, which don't always affect the runtime if the actual product binaries were deployed correctly. However, don't trust this — InstallCache is used for future patching/repair, and a corrupted cache will cause the next patch to fail in unpredictable ways. You did the right thing reverting.

Recommended plan

  1. Snapshot the VM.
  2. Reboot.
  3. Verify CrowdStrike is fully gone (services, drivers, files).
  4. Disable Defender real-time protection, ASR, and Controlled Folder Access via PowerShell.
  5. Stop Windows Search and any backup agent.
  6. Disconnect the network.
  7. Start Process Monitor with a filter for ACCESS DENIED.
  8. Run the upgrade.
  9. If it still fails, ProcMon will tell you exactly which process is responsible.

If after all this it still fails with no third-party process visible in ProcMon, then it becomes worth re-engaging SAP support with the ProcMon trace as evidence — at that point the problem is genuinely inside the installer.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 04 - Namespaces, contexts and name prefixes
Re: ❓ Question
entry.content:
After you have exited a Payroll Period, you have only 2 options to process data that should have been processed in it before "Exit Payroll".

 1 - Wait until the next Payroll Period is processed, and the retroactive changes will be taken into account;

 2 - Do an Off-Cycle Pay to adress the problem if you need immediate resolution.

entry.title: Re: Rework order
entry.content: Configure QM follow‑up actions so that rework orders are automatically created and reference the parent production order. Use order type RWR (or equivalent) for rework, and ensure the parent order number is captured in the reference field. This setup allows you to track rework quantities, costs, and inspection results back to the original production order.

entry.title: Having issue with the storage locations for plant XXXX
entry.content:
I wanted to reach out regarding the storage locations for plant XXXX in our development system. Currently, I am unable to see the full list of storage locations—there are only 6 available, whereas in the quality system, we have 27. I've already checked the change log and confirmed that no changes have been made for the plant. I also looked into the table itself to see if any data had been deleted, but there is no indication of missing data.

Could you please help me understand how these storage locations might have been deleted from the development system? Any insights or guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you for your support.

entry.title: Guiding your customer through the new API policy and ODP-RFC
entry.content:
The way customers connect to SAP systems is changing, and you can feel it in almost every integration discussion.

This session, “Guiding your customer through the new API policy and ODP-RFC”, helps you make sense of that shift and turn it into something practical for your day-to-day work.

SAP is refining how data is accessed to improve security and consistency, especially as AI and automation become part of more solutions. Plus, topics like ODP-RFC scope and non-SAP access are getting clearer boundaries.

Honestly, the thing is, many customers will not notice the policy changes first. They will notice integration behavior, rate limits, or new constraints. That is where your guidance matters.

We will cover governed data access, SAP Business Data Cloud for non-SAP scenarios, and the April 26, 2026 API policy. You will also see how Agent2Agent fits into modern integration patterns and how to plan migrations step by step.

If you are in consulting, sales, or architecture, this session gives you a clear way to support customer conversations with confidence.

Register now and join the discussion. https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/may/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00014315.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPIntegration #DataGovernance

entry.title: Re: stock removal not happening while doing MIGO because...
entry.content:
This scenario should not occur, the flag cannot be set/unset if

  • Stocks already exist
  • Stocks already exist at plant level
  • Stocks already exist for the sales order
  • Value-only materials
  • SD documents exists
  • Batches already exist
  • WM stocks (quants) already exist

Read this KBA 3225497 - FAQ: How-to change batch management flag when error message M3530 appears

  • Use the described scenarios to restore normal operations.
  • Execute an inventory clearance, potentially reverting to the prior status, followed by status adjustment and stock reposting.

entry.title: Re: copying dylib into AppBundle and licensing
entry.content:
You cannot include a Developer Edition component in an AppBundle and "put it out".  

The Developer Edition License Agreement addresses this specific question. Here are excerpts

NOTE: ALTHOUGH SQL ANYWHERE DEVELOPER EDITION MAY BE AVAILABLE AT NO CHARGE, THE PROGRAM IS NOT TRANSFERABLE. THEREFORE, YOU AGREE NOT TO DISTRIBUTE, SUBLICENSE, OR OTHERWISE TRANSFER THE PROGRAM TO (OR ALLOW THE USE OF THE PROGRAM BY) ANY THIRD PARTY.

2. Unbundling. As noted above, SAP Sybase SQL Anywhere Developer Edition is a suite of several component software products. Except as otherwise specifically authorized below, use of all components of this Program is limited to the same development Seat(s) (e.g., PC, single user workstation, or real time device) for which you acquired this Program license, and the components of this Program may not be unbundled and used by different or additional Seat(s).

3. Use of Programs.
a. Developer Use of Programs. Each copy of this SQL Anywhere Developer Edition is licensed solely for internal evaluation, development and testing of applications compatible with the Program. ...

c. No Other Rights Granted. Deployment licenses for any other purpose (including, without limitation, usage in a general production environment or on a webserver) must be obtained from Sybase before you deploy or make any other use of SQL Anywhere Developer Edition or any component thereof. You may not transfer or sublicense the Program or any component thereof to any third parties.

entry.title: Re: IMS LTI integration
entry.content:
Hi Krishna,

Short answer: SuccessFactors Learning doesn't support LTI natively. The feature has been requested but hasn't been developed yet (see this thread for SAP's own confirmation: https://community.sap.com/t5/human-capital-management-q-a/succesfactors-learning-support-lti/qaq-p/12064590). Your vote on the open influence.sap.com idea might help get it prioritized.

The workaround that works in practice is to publish the LTI content as a SCORM 1.2 package, since SCORM is what SF launches natively. We do that LTI-to-SCORM bridging for our clients, so the content runs inside SF with SSO and completion tracking back in Learning. More detail here: https://linqur.com/en/lti-sap-successfactors

The approach sounds a bit roundabout, but the end result is your LTI content running inside SuccessFactors, and it can be set up in minutes. 

(Disclosure: I work at Linqur.)

entry.title: SAP Community Voice: ABAP New Era, Get to know SAP Champions and Enhancements in our platform!
entry.content:
June is bringing exciting innovations, community updates, and plenty of opportunities to connect and learn across the SAP ecosystem. In this edition, we highlight some of the latest developments for ABAP developers following the announcements at SAP Sapphire 2026, practical guidance from SAP Community experts, and new SAP Community enhancements designed to improve your experience on the platform.  

We also celebrate the introduction of the New SAP Champions, share all you need to know to participate in the latest Ask Me Anything session, and invite you to join upcoming community events taking place around the world.

Check now!

Featured Blog Posts 

Entering the New Era of Agentic AI for ABAP Development 

Following the SAP Sapphire 2026 announcement around ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code, SAP's Head of ABAP Platform, Sonja Liénard, published a blog post sharing more details about the broader vision behind these innovations. The post highlights a series of major announcements that mark what SAP describes as the most significant step forward in ABAP AI since the introduction of SAP Joule for Developers. Among the key updates are the general availability of the ABAP MCP Server, opening ABAP development to agentic AI assistants and coding agents, the upcoming release of ABAP Development Tools for VS Code, new AI-powered custom code migration agents for SAP S/4HANA transformations, expanded availability of ABAP AI capabilities for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition customers, and a new consumption-based commercial model. Together, these announcements reflect SAP's strategy to make ABAP development more intelligent, accessible, and integrated with the growing AI ecosystem, while helping developers focus more on business value and less on repetitive tasks. 

Read Sonja Blog Post here 

The Future of ABAP: VS Code ADT, Zero-Config MCP, and AI Co-pilots 

Building on the announcements highlighted above, SAP Community member Lukshmi Podile takes a closer look at one of the most significant updates for ABAP developers: the general availability of ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code. In her blog post, Lukshmi explores how the new solution goes beyond being an alternative to Eclipse by introducing a modern, file-based architecture powered by the ABAP Language Server and the ABAP MCP (Model Context Protocol) Server, enabling AI-assisted and agentic development scenarios. The post explains how developers can use VS Code to work with RAP and ABAP Cloud objects, run debugging, ABAP Unit Tests, and ATC checks, while also connecting AI copilots such as SAP Joule for Developers and GitHub Copilot directly to their ABAP development environment. In addition to covering the key capabilities, Lukshmi provides a complete step-by-step setup guide to help developers get started with this new development experience. 

Read Lukshmi Blog Post here 

Controlling Excel Exports in SAP Analytics Cloud  

One of the most viewed and liked blog posts recently published in the Technology Blog Posts area comes from SAP Community member Sebastian Bock. Using a fun and relatable "Excel-Ernst" storyline, Sebastian walks through the different ways SAP Analytics Cloud users may attempt to export data to Excel and, more importantly, how administrators can control and restrict those options. The blog covers everything from disabling story and widget exports to managing Data Analyzer access, Excel Add-In usage, and Datasphere permissions. Beyond the technical settings, it also highlights an important governance principle: the strongest protection is thoughtful dashboard design that limits exposure of sensitive data from the start. Based on the engagement and positive feedback in the comments, this practical guide clearly resonated with many of our members. If you have not read it yet, we highly recommend checking it out. 

Read Sebastian Blog Post here 

Meet our New SAP Champions 

Last month, we officially welcomed 10 outstanding experts to the SAP Champions program. We are excited to see what they will bring to our SAP Community and grateful for the warm support you have shown while welcoming them across our groups and social channels. Being an SAP Champion means taking an active role in supporting community members, helping them get the most out of their SAP Community experience, sharing insights and expertise through blog posts, answering questions, contributing to discussions, and leading community-driven initiatives. To help everyone get to know our new Champions better, the Head of SAP Champions Program Mynyna Chau has published a series of blog posts in the SAP Community Leaders Finder group, introducing each of them individually. With this content, you can learn more about their areas of expertise, daily work, motivations, and what inspired them to become SAP Champions. Plus, at the end of each blog post, you will find a question from the featured Champion to the community. If you have an answer, be sure to join the conversation and share your thoughts in the comments! 

Read the New SAP Champions Blog Posts here 

Community News 

SAP Community Enhancements 

Over the past few months, our team has been working on a series of updates designed to improve the SAP Community experience. From better content discovery and navigation to clearer visibility of new activity, here are some of the latest enhancements now available: 

Clearer Profile Navigation 

  • “My Account” is now labeled “My SAP Profile” 
  • “My content” is now labeled “My Community Profile” 

These changes make it easier to distinguish between your SAP account settings and your community activity. 

Improved Search Experience 

  • The experience previously known as “Beta search” is now officially called AI Search (SAP’s Intelligent Search) 
  • AI Search is now the default search experience in public areas 
  • You can still switch between search options using the dropdown 

This update helps you find more relevant content faster and with improved accuracy. 

Smarter Content Discovery 

  • The Recent Activity feed on the homepage now defaults to “Most Commented” 
  • This makes it easier to quickly find active and engaging discussions 

Consistent “Like” Experience 

  • Continued updates replace “Kudos” with “Likes” across the platform 
  • Fixes improve consistency in search, notifications, and page labeling 

Improved Events Experience 

  • Event times in the calendar month view are now displayed correctly 
  • Times are consistent across views and reflect your browser time zone 

Better Notification Emails 

  • Fixed issues with unsubscribe links in certain emails such as badge notifications and group invitations 
  • You can now reliably manage your notification settings directly from these emails 

UI and Layout Improvements 

  • Improved spacing around Subscribe buttons for better usability 
  • Additional small layout fixes improve readability and consistency 

Performance Enhancements 

  • Optimized the floating indicator to reduce background processing 
  • This improves page speed and responsiveness 

Platform Update 

  • Upgrade to Khoros Communities 26.2, bringing general stability and performance improvements 

What all this means for you?

  • Easier navigation between SAP profile and community profile 
  • Faster, more relevant search results 
  • More engaging and timely content in feeds 
  • Clearer and more consistent terminology 
  • Improved reliability in events and notifications 
  • Better overall performance 

As always, please make sure to try out these updates and give us your feedback!  

SAP Champion Ask Me Anything – Session 4 

As we have highlighted in previous editions, the Ask Me Anything series with our SAP Champions is a fantastic opportunity to learn directly from experienced SAP experts and receive deeper, more detailed answers than what is usually possible through comments alone. Rather than a quick reply in a discussion thread, participants receive thoughtful responses through dedicated video answers, giving you a more complete view of the expert's knowledge. This month, the spotlight is on SAP Champion Sergio Guerrero, an SAP HANA and SAP Fiori specialist, author of two books covering Fiori applications and microservices in XSA, and an active contributor to SAP community events such as SAP Inside Track, CodeJams, Stammtisch gathering. Sergio's AMA session is dedicated to SAP Fiori topics, including SAPUI5, Fiori applications on HANA XSA, CAP, and S/4HANA. Community members can submit their questions directly in the comments of his blog post here or anonymously through the Forms here until June 30, 2026. After the submission period closes, Sergio will record video responses addressing the selected questions. Whether you have a specific technical challenge, want to learn from his years of experience in Fiori development and HANA modeling, or simply want to say hello and connect, this is a great chance to engage. 

Events 

SAP Charlotte Innovation Showcase 2026 

This month SAP Community Charlotte is excited to host the SAP Charlotte Innovation Showcase 2026 on June 19, a virtual event open to everyone, focused on SAP solutions, SAP BTP architectures, AI-enabled use cases, and real-world transformation stories. The format includes up to 10 speakers, selected on a first-come, first-served basis, with each session consisting of a 15-minute presentation followed by a 5-minute Q&A. Topics can cover SAP BTP, S/4HANA, AI, Event-Driven Architecture, Integration Suite, Datasphere, SAP Analytics Cloud, industry solutions, and more. The organizers are currently looking for speakers and invite SAP architects, consultants, developers, and industry experts to submit their proposals. To apply, participants need to provide a title, a short abstract, speaker bio, SAP technologies used, and industry focus (Speakers Application Forms Here). Presenters will have the opportunity to showcase their innovations to the community, receive feedback from a panel of expert judges, and compete for the SAP Charlotte Innovator Award, with certificates provided to all speakers. If you have an interesting SAP project, implementation, architecture, or transformation story to share, this is a fantastic opportunity to get involved. Community members who prefer to attend can also register to learn from the sessions, support the speakers, and connect with fellow SAP professionals (Participation Registration Forms Here). 

Lisbon Summer Meetup 

Are you around Lisbon this month? Because there is an incredible opportunity to take part in the second edition of the SAP Community Meetup Lisbon. This Summer Edition will take place on June 18, 2026, from 6:30 PM to 8:00 PM CEST at Jardim Maria José Moura in Lisbon, Portugal. The event is for community members that are looking to expand their network, and exchange ideas in a relaxed and inspiring summer atmosphere. This informal gathering encourages participants to share experiences, discuss innovation, learn from one another, and strengthen connections within the local SAP ecosystem. Whether you are already active in the community or looking to meet fellow members for the first time, this is a great chance to engage with the SAP Community in Portugal! Register now here. 

SAP CodeJam and SAP Inside Track in Weinheim 

If you are already planning your SAP Community activities for September, consider joining two community events in Weinheim, Germany! On September 18, 2026, SAP Developer Advocate DJ Adams (@qmacro) will host SAP CodeJam focused on CAP CDS modelling at the Syntax Auditorium in Weinheim (register for the CodeJam here). The organizers are also hosting an informal community get-together on that evening for speakers, attendees, and anyone already in town. On the following day, September 19, the first SAP Inside Track Weinheim will take place at the same venue, for a full day of community-led sessions, networking, and knowledge sharing (register for the Inside Track here). The organizers are currently looking for speakers for the SIT (register to be a Speaker at the SIT here). and community members are invited to submit sessions on a wide range of SAP topics. First-time speakers are especially encouraged to participate, with session lengths available in both 20- and 40-minute formats. Please make sure to read the Event Organizer Max Streifeneder full blog post here for more details.

entry.title: Overtime Calculation when employee records time on 6th and 7th day
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

We have a customer requirement that the employee is paid overtime for hours worked beyond 40 hours in a week. However, if the employee recorded a time on the 6th and 7th day, they should automatically be considered overtime. 

For example, the employee recorded time (regardless of if it is rostered day or not) on Monday, Wed-Sunday - then Sunday is the 6th day so whatever the hours entered on that day will be considered overtime, regardless is the total number of hours worked for that week is less than 40 hours.

If the employee worked Wednesday- Sunday, he only worked for 5 days. Therefore, no OT payment for 6th or 7th day.

This is a bit tricky because we can't just use Saturday and Sunday as filters to fetch the overtime hours, but it has to be the 6th and 7th day worked.

We tried to use counted events but it's not consistently fetching the 6th and 7th day.

Any ideas, how can we achieve this requirement?

entry.title: SAP Subscription Billing × S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 連携（57Z）— 前提知識と全体アーキテクチャ
entry.content:
はじめに

近年、製造業をはじめとする多くの業界でサブスクリプションビジネスへの転換が加速しています。たとえば、産業機械メーカーが従来の「機械本体の単品販売」に加え、以下のような複合的な収益モデルを展開するケースが増えています。

課金種別内容例在庫管理処理システム
一回限り
（One-time）		機械本体の販売50万円/台あり
（在庫品）		S/4HANA Cloud Sales
一回限り
（One-time）		セットアップ費用など
（※本シナリオ対象外）		—なし
（非在庫品）		SAP Subscription Billing
定期課金
（Recurring）		IoTサービス月額料金3万円/月なし
（非在庫品）		SAP Subscription Billing
従量課金
（Usage-based）		稼働時間に応じた加算料金500円/時間なし
（非在庫品）		SAP Subscription Billing

このようなハイブリッド課金モデルを既存のSAP S/4HANA Cloud環境に統合するための標準ソリューションが、SAP Best Practicesスコープアイテム 57Z「Subscription Management with Sales Billing」 です。

本記事では、57Z連携の概要・システムアーキテクチャ・データフローの前提知識を解説します。各機能の詳細設定手順については次回以降の記事で取り上げます。

57Z とは

57ZはSAP Subscription BillingとSAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition（以降、S/4HANA Cloud）を統合するためのSAP Best Practicesスコープアイテムです。

Lead to Cashのエンドツーエンドビジネスプロセス「Solution Business for Cloud Deployment」の一部として位置づけられており、以下の3つの基本プロセスを実現します。

  1. サブスクリプションの生成 — S/4HANA CloudのAPIまたはソリューション受注からSubscription Billingにサブスクリプションを作成
  2. 使用量収集とレーティング — 従量課金・定期課金・一回限り課金の自動計算
  3. 請求データ転送 — Subscription BillingからS/4HANA CloudのSales Billingへ請求データを連携し、顧客請求書を発行

システムアーキテクチャ — 連携サービス全体像

57Z連携を構成するコンポーネントと、それぞれの役割を理解することがアーキテクチャ設計の第一歩です。ドキュメントに記載されている連携図（Integrated Data）を以下で解説します。

コンポーネントと役割

コンポーネント役割
SAP Subscription Billingサブスクリプション・請求予測・請求データの管理とレーティング
SAP Master Data Integration（MDI）S/4HANA CloudからSubscription Billingへのマスタデータ同期（推奨）
SAP Cloud Application Event Hubサブスクリプション・請求予測のイベント連携。Subscription BillingとS/4HANA Cloudが同一BTPリージョン内にある場合に使用（推奨）
SAP Event Mesh両システムが異なるBTPリージョンにある場合（例：EUとAPJ）のイベント連携代替手段（defaultプラン）
SAP Cloud Integrationマスタデータ連携・請求データ連携の旧方式（Deprecated／非推奨）
SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Editionソリューション受注管理・Sales Billing・財務処理

重要： SAP Cloud IntegrationのIntegration Flowによるマスタデータ連携は現在非推奨（Deprecated）です。新規プロジェクトでは MDI（マスタデータ）＋ Event Hub（イベント） の構成を採用してください。

データフローの全体像

マスタデータ連携（MDI経由）： MDIを介したマスタデータ連携には2つの方向があります。ビジネスパートナはS/4HANA CloudからSubscription Billingへ顧客として同期されます。一方、製品・料金要素はSubscription BillingからS/4HANA Cloudへ購読請求製品（SBPD）・購読請求レート要素（SBRE）として連携されます。

ソリューション受注との連携： S/4HANA CloudのSolution OrdersからSubscription Billingへサブスクリプションが作成され、逆方向にはサブスクリプションのシミュレーション結果がSolution Ordersに返されます。

トランザクションデータ連携（Event Hub経由）： Subscription Billingで生成されたサブスクリプションと請求予測が、Business EventsとしてS/4HANA Cloud側のプロバイダー契約・請求プランに反映されます。

請求データ連携（ダイレクト連携）： Subscription BillingのBillsが、S/4HANA CloudのBilling Document Requests（SBDR）としてダイレクトに連携されます。

まとめ

  • 57Zは一回限り・定期・従量の複合課金モデルをSAP標準で実現するスコープアイテム
  • マスタデータ同期にはMDI、イベント連携にはEvent Hubを使用するのが現在の推奨構成
  • SAP Cloud IntegrationのIntegration Flowは非推奨であり、新規実装では使用しない
  • Event HubとEvent Meshの選択はBTPリージョンの構成に依存する

次回以降の記事では、以下の各プロセスの詳細設定手順を順番に解説していきます。

  1. ビジネスパートナ登録（MDIによるマスタデータ連携）
  2. 料金要素登録（Rate Element）
  3. 料金プランテンプレート登録
  4. 製品登録（Product Configuration in Subscription Billing）
  5. サブスクリプション登録・請求（Sales Billingへのデータ転送）

entry.title: Re: Cannot migrate AuC during fiscal year
entry.content:
Hi Paaa,

The errors FAA_POST 504 ("Cumul. values not allowed as asset value date is in transfer year") and AA 322 ("Value date is earlier than capitalization date") occur due to standard Legacy Data Transfer (LDT) validation logic in SAP Asset Accounting.

Your transfer date is April 30, 2026, which makes 2026 your current transfer year. You are attempting to migrate values for AUC001 and AUC002 with capitalization/value dates in January and February 2026 using the "Cumulative Values" structure.

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Cumulative Values are strictly reserved for historical asset balances accumulated up to the end of the last closed fiscal year (e.g., December 31, 2025). Any costs incurred between the start of the transfer year and the mid-year transfer date (January 1, 2026 – April 30, 2026) are classified as Current Year Acquisitions (or transactions during the year of transfer) and cannot be migrated as cumulative legacy balances.

Technical Solution

To resolve this issue within the SAP S/4HANA Migration Cockpit (app "Migrate Your Data"):

  1. Clear Cumulative Fields: Remove all data entries for AUC001 and AUC002 from the Cumulative Values sheet in your XML migration template.

  2. Use Transaction Sheets: Move the migration data for these two AuCs to the Transaction Header and Transaction Items sheets.

  3. Map Dates Correctly: Populate the amounts as current year transactions. Ensure the Asset Value Date used in the transaction item exactly matches or is later than the Capitalization Date (e.g., 01/06/2026 for AUC001).

Please check these notes describing your issue: 3309477 and Transfer During the Fiscal Year 

Best Regards,

Michal

entry.title: Re: Production order change log.
entry.content:
Good day @sriny1081 

Thank you for your question.

In the Public Cloud, there is no standard production order change history report that shows all changes such as changed yield quantity, component quantity, added or removed components, User ID, date, time, etc. See KBA 3664613 for more details.

In simple terms:

  • There is no standard report in Public Cloud for full production order change logs.
  • The Display Changes functionality does not provide production order change history in the way you require.
  • IMG based activation or SAP Note 390635 is an on-premise or private cloud type approach, not a Public Cloud configuration option.
  • Order Change Management is not something you can activate step by step in Public Cloud through IMG.
  • You may consider raising an improvement request.

 

The Public Cloud only offers the possibility to check available order information through apps such as
Manage Production Orders, 

Display Production Order, Monitor Production Order or Process Orders

  

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Is LLM fine-tuning really dead?
entry.content:
@Marla-Ellinger 

Fine-tuning isn’t really dead, but it is definitely not the default answer for every problem anymore. In many cases, good prompting, retrieval, and context engineering can get you further with less effort and less complexity. That said, fine-tuning still makes sense when you need very consistent structured outputs, a specialized domain behavior, or a smaller model that has to run efficiently. The real question is not whether fine-tuning is dead — it is whether it is the right tool for the specific job.

entry.title: Re: Improve Your Scrum Process with AI
entry.content:
@_Dimitri_ 

AI can make Scrum a lot smoother, but only if it is used to support the team rather than replace the team’s judgment. The biggest value I see is in reducing the manual work around summaries, backlog cleanup, blocker detection, and sprint reporting so the team can spend more time on real collaboration. When used well, AI does not change the purpose of Scrum — it simply removes some of the noise around it.

entry.title: Re: SAP Analytics Cloud Connection To Sharepoint via Open Connectors
entry.content:
Hi Jiri - from what I understand the fix is still in flight with SAP in Openconnectors but there is a workaround.

1. In SAC Create a new connection of type SharePoint - give it a name different than any instance's in Openconnectors
2. Set the site address to xxxxx.sharepoint.com (replace with your company root URL)
3. Use the same app ID and secret as is used in Openconnectors - this is the one that uses the https://bocauth.us1.sapbusinessobjects.cloud:443 redirect
Set MS Oauth to AllSites.Read and the Scope = true
Save the SAC connection
Open Open connectors and F5 the instance screen.
Observe that the connection you just made in SAC is seen as an instance in Openconnectors.
Adjust the site address of the Openconnectors instance that was just created to be the full path of xxxxx.sharepoint.com/sites/xxxxxxxxxx - the full path of the site you want to hit.
Put in the secret for your app - the one using the https://bocauth.us1.sapbusinessobjects.cloud:443 redirect (the one you used in your SAC connection)
Press Update - DO NOT RE-AUTHENTICATE
Open the connection again in SAC and observe that the site address in the connection has changed to be xxxxx.sharepoint.com/sites/xxxxxxxxxx - which should be the full path you just put in in Openconnectors.

Test your SAC connection

Hope this helps

entry.title: Design Question – Compensation vs Variable Pay
entry.content:
HI Expert,

I have a design question regarding an AIP requirement and would appreciate your thoughts.

The client has indicated that AIP is generally based on the employee's final salary and is prorated based on hire date. However, there is one exception: if an employee moves from a Non-Executive employment type to an Executive employment type during the performance year, the AIP is prorated based on the time spent in each employment type. The target bonus and the use of individual performance may also differ between Non-Executive and Executive employees.

Salary changes, FTE changes, and other events do not generate proration; only hire date and the Non-Executive to Executive employment type change.

Based on this requirement, would you still consider Compensation as the best fit, or would you lean toward Variable Pay due to the historical employment type proration? Also, from a Variable Pay perspective, could we use a single salary basis (final salary) and only prorate by employment type history, or would salary history also need to be considered?

entry.title: Re: Product Adhoc Movement from Resource EWM
entry.content:
I encountered the same problem. Perhaps it's too late for you, but for anyone who could experience the same situation, the key is to move the product to a bin that admits materials without HU, like a GI-ZONE (Goods Issue Zone). A bin-to-bin transfer using the Warehouse Process Type S999 allows you to move the stock out of the resource, and then you can pack the product (/SCWM/PACK) once you've confirmed the WT. 

To create the WT, you've to go to Warehouse Monitor -> Stock and Bin -> Resources, select the Resource that is keeping your precious product and then select Available Stock. You'll be able to create the WT there.

 

Sorry the captures are in Spanish, but you get the key elements!

Hope it can help 🙂

entry.title: Calling Claude from ABAP: How I Wired SAP AI Core into a Real Business Scenario
entry.content:
The Itch I Had to Scratch

I was working on a module where users needed to search through hundreds of business roles using a classic SAP filter panel — dropdowns, input fields, coded values. Fine. But I kept thinking: what if someone could just type what they want in plain English?
 
"Show me high-sensitivity finance roles in production."
 
That sentence maps to at least four filter fields, each with a coded value the user has no reason to know. The UI couldn't do it. The roadmap wasn't going to do it soon either. So I decided to find out whether I could wire SAP AI Core — specifically Claude — directly into ABAP, in an afternoon, as a proof of concept. The answer was yes. Here's how it works, and more importantly, why the pattern is useful far beyond my specific use case. SAP AI Core exposes a REST API. ABAP can make HTTP calls via CL_HTTP_CLIENT using SM59 RFC destinations. AI Core uses OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials for authentication.
 
So, the flow is:
 
ABAP → OAuth token endpoint (SM59 destination) → get bearer token → ABAP → AI Core inference endpoint (second SM59 destination) → send prompt → parse JSON response back into ABAP structures.
 
That's it. No middleware, no BTP sidecar, no external service. Pure ABAP.
 
Step 1: Two SM59 Destinations
 
Everything starts in SM59 (Transaction for RFC/HTTP destination configuration). You need two HTTP destinations:
 
AI_CORE_TOKEN — OAuth 2.0 token endpoint
AI_CORE_API — Model inference endpoint
 
The token destination needs no authentication (you'll pass credentials in the body). The API destination uses the bearer token you fetch at runtime. Both use HTTPS — make sure your SAP system has the correct SSL certificates imported via STRUST.
 
Note: The deployment ID in the API path is specific to your AI Core deployment. In my case I was using Claude via Bedrock through AI Core's proxy.
 
Step 2: Fetching the OAuth Token in ABAP
 
The OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials flow is straightforward: POST your client_id and client_secret as form-encoded body, get back a JSON response containing access_token.
 
METHOD get_bearer_token.
  DATA lo_http TYPE REF TO if_http_client.

  cl_http_client=>create_by_destination(
    EXPORTING destination = 'AI_CORE_TOKEN'
    IMPORTING client      = lo_http
    EXCEPTIONS OTHERS     = 6 ).

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

  lo_http->request->set_method(
    if_http_request=>co_request_method_post ).
  lo_http->request->set_header_field(
    name  = 'Content-Type'
    value = 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded' ).

  " Client credentials flow
  DATA(lv_body) =
    |grant_type=client_credentials| &
    |&client_id={ c_client_id }| &
    |&client_secret={ c_client_secret }| &
    |&resource_tenant_id={ c_tenant_id }|.

  lo_http->request->set_cdata( lv_body ).
  lo_http->send( ).
  lo_http->receive( ).

  DATA(lv_response) = lo_http->response->get_cdata( ).
  lo_http->close( ).

  " Extract token from JSON
  FIND FIRST OCCURRENCE OF REGEX
    '"access_token"\s*:\s*"([^"]+)"'
    IN lv_response
    SUBMATCHES rv_token.
ENDMETHOD.
 
Step 3: Calling the Model
 
With a token in hand, calling the inference endpoint is a standard HTTP POST. The important detail for Claude is the request body format — Claude uses a system / messages split rather than a single flat prompt array:
METHOD call_ai_core.
  DATA lo_http TYPE REF TO if_http_client.

  cl_http_client=>create_by_destination(
    EXPORTING destination = 'AI_CORE_API'
    IMPORTING client      = lo_http
    EXCEPTIONS OTHERS     = 6 ).

  lo_http->request->set_method( 'POST' ).
  lo_http->request->set_header_field(
    name  = 'Authorization'
    value = |Bearer { iv_token }| ).
  lo_http->request->set_header_field(
    name  = 'Content-Type'
    value = 'application/json' ).
  lo_http->request->set_header_field(
    name  = 'AI-Resource-Group'
    value = 'default' ).

  DATA(lv_body) =
    |\{| &
    |"anthropic_version":"bedrock-2023-05-31",| &
    |"max_tokens":1024,| &
    |"system":"{ escape_for_json( build_system_prompt( ) ) }",| &
    |"messages":[| &
    |  \{"role":"user","content":"{ escape_for_json( iv_prompt ) }"\}| &
    |]| &
    |\}|.

  lo_http->request->set_cdata( lv_body ).
  lo_http->send( ).
  lo_http->receive( ).

  rv_result = lo_http->response->get_cdata( ).
  lo_http->close( ).
ENDMETHOD.
 
The AI-Resource-Group header is mandatory — AI Core uses it to route to the correct deployment context.
 
Step 4: The Prompt Engineering Part (Where the Real Work Is)
 
This is where I spent most of my time, and where I think the most interesting learning sits.
 
The goal: Given a free-text user query, return a predictable, structured JSON object that my ABAP code can deserialize directly into a typed structure.
 
The technique is called constrained structured output via prompt engineering. You tell the model exactly what schema to follow, exactly what coded values are valid, and you instruct it to return only valid JSON — no markdown, no preamble.
 
A simplified version of my system prompt:
 
You are a filter interpreter for an SAP application.
Convert natural language queries into structured JSON filter parameters.

Rules:
- Only use the exact filter fields and values listed below.
- For coded fields use the exact codes shown.
- Today is [injected at runtime via sy-datum].
- Return ONLY valid JSON. No markdown. No text outside JSON.

=== AVAILABLE FILTER FIELDS ===

Field: "role_type"
Type: CODED
Values:
  "SIN" = Single Role
  "COM" = Composite Role
  "BUS" = Business Role

=== OUTPUT FORMAT ===
{
  "filters": [
    {"field": "role_type", "operator": "EQ", "value": "SIN", "label": "Role Type = Single"}
  ],
  "interpretation": "Plain English summary of what was understood",
  "confidence": 90,
  "warnings": "Any assumptions, or empty string"
}
 
Step 5: Parsing the Response Back Into ABAP
 
The Claude response wraps the model output in a JSON envelope:
{
  "content": [
    {"type": "text", "text": "{...your structured JSON...}"}
  ]
}

 

I extract the inner JSON with a regex, unescape it, then deserialize it with /UI2/CL_JSON:
METHOD extract_json_from_response.
  FIND FIRST OCCURRENCE OF REGEX
    '"text"\s*:\s*"((?:[^"\\]|\\.)*)"'
    IN iv_raw_response
    SUBMATCHES rv_json.

  REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF '\"' IN rv_json WITH '"'.
  REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF '\n' IN rv_json WITH ' '.
  REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF '\\' IN rv_json WITH '\'.
ENDMETHOD.

 

Then the deserialization:
/ui2/cl_json=>deserialize(
  EXPORTING json        = lv_json
            pretty_name = /ui2/cl_json=>pretty_mode-camel_case
  CHANGING  data        = ls_result ).
 
/UI2/CL_JSON handles camelCase to snake_case mapping automatically when you use pretty_mode-camel_case. No manual field mapping needed.
 
The Result
A single public method call:
DATA(ls_result) = zcl_nl_filter_poc=>parse_nl_to_filter(
  iv_query = 'Show me high sensitivity composite roles in the production release' ).

IF ls_result-success = abap_true.
  " ls_result-filter_json     -> ready to apply as filter conditions
  " ls_result-interpretation  -> show user what was understood
  " ls_result-confidence      -> 0-100, warn if below threshold
  " ls_result-warnings        -> surface assumptions to user
ENDIF.
 
Wiring It Into a Fiori App
The class works as a standalone utility, but the real payoff comes when you surface it in a real UI. I exposed it through a RAP custom action on an OData V4 service — the action receives the natural language query as a parameter, calls parse_nl_to_filter, and writes the result back to the entity.
 
On the Fiori side, the controller calls invokeAction on the binding context, passes the search field value, and OData V4 side effects take care of refreshing the UI automatically once the action returns. No manual model refresh needed. 
 
And this is how it looked like!!
 

 

Final Thought
The thing that surprised me most was how little plumbing was needed. Two SM59 destinations, one HTTP client call for auth, one for inference, a regex to unpack the response. The bulk of the work was the prompt itself — which is a different kind of engineering than I'm used to, but a genuinely interesting one.
 
If you're an ABAP developer wondering whether AI is "for you" or only for the frontend teams and architects with BTP budgets — it's for you too. The tools are already there.

entry.title: Solving SmartRecruiter's Salary Calculation Gap for Offer Letters Using SAP Integration Suite (CPI)
entry.content:
The Business Problem

In many organizations, offer letters must present a full salary breakdown — Cost to Company (CTC), Basic salary, HRA, and Special Allowance — each derived from the gross CTC using pre-defined calculation logic. SmartRecruiter, as a standard product, does not natively support this on-the-fly salary computation. Recruiting stakeholders are left to manually populate individual salary components, which introduces risk of error and inconsistency.

The requirement is straightforward: when a recruiter enters the CTC for a candidate, the system should automatically calculate the salary components and pre-populate the offer fields — without any manual intervention and without a custom build inside SmartRecruiter.

Solution Overview

The solution leverages the Webhook capability native to SmartRecruiter and the orchestration power of SAP Integration Suite. The approach is event-driven: when a recruiter updates the CTC field (or moves a candidate to the next stage), a webhook fires and triggers an integration flow in CPI that performs the salary calculation and upserts the results back to SmartRecruiter via REST APIs.

Webhook Setup

Two webhook events are subscribed to in SmartRecruiter to cover both update scenarios:

  • application.fields.updated — fires when a field value on an application is changed
  • application.status.updated — fires when the candidate is moved to a new stage

Either event reliably signals that the CTC may have been updated and that the calculation should run. The webhook payload carries the candidate ID and job requisition ID, which are used as keys in subsequent API call

Step 1 — Configuration in SmartRecruiter

Custom Application Fields

CTC -

HRA -

Similarly define other pay components required for calculations -

CTC as application field.

Rest of pay components as offer fields.

For ex - Four custom fields are configured in SmartRecruiter to hold the salary components:

  • CTC — Gross Cost to Company (input field; marked as mandatory for the recruiter to populate)
  • HRA — House Rent Allowance (output; auto-populated via CPI)
  • Basic — Basic Salary (output; auto-populated via CPI)
  • Special Allowance — (output; auto-populated via CPI)

Since CTC is the trigger field, it is set as mandatory. The recruiting stakeholder is instructed to update CTC either at the application stage or when moving the candidate to the offer stage.

Recruiting stakeholder fills the value of CTC field -

Step 2 — Retrieving the CTC Value from SmartRecruiter

How Fields Are Stored in SmartRecruiter

In SmartRecruiter, custom fields are represented as candidate properties — effectively key-value pairs attached to the candidate entity. Each field has a unique Property ID (UUID) that is referenced in API calls.

Retrieving Candidate Properties

To retrieve all property values for a candidate, including the CTC field, the following GET request is used:

How to retrieve the CTC value - 

 

Step 3 — Salary Calculation in SAP Integration Suite (CPI)

Once the webhook fires and the CPI iFlow is triggered, the integration flow performs the following sequence:

  • Extracts the candidate ID and job requisition ID from the webhook payload
  • Calls the SmartRecruiter properties API to fetch the current CTC value for the candidate
  • Applies the pre-configured salary calculation logic within the Groovy script or message mapping step in CPI
  • Derives HRA, Basic, and Special Allowance values from the CTC
  • Prepares the upsert payload with the computed values
Integration Note

The calculation logic (e.g., percentage splits for HRA, Basic, Special Allowance) is externalized in CPI and can be adjusted without touching SmartRecruiter configuration. This keeps the business rules in a governed, version-controlled integration layer.

Step 4 — Upserting Calculated Values Back to SmartRecruiter

With the calculated salary components ready, CPI writes the values back to the SmartRecruiter candidate record using a PUT/PATCH call scoped to both the candidate and the specific job requisition:

The payload specifies each Property ID and its newly calculated value. All three output fields — HRA, Basic, and Special Allowance — are upserted in a single call.

Step 5 — Verifying the Results

After the upsert, a GET call to the candidate properties endpoint confirms that the calculated values are now stored correctly:

Step 6 — Creating the Offer in SmartRecruiter

Once the candidate properties are populated, the recruiter proceeds to create the formal offer letter inside SmartRecruiter. Because the salary components have already been written to the candidate record, the offer template picks them up automatically — the recruiter sees pre-populated values for all compensation fields, including Special Allowance, without any manual calculation.

Handling Renegotiation Scenarios

Real-world recruiting frequently involves renegotiation. If a candidate requests a revised offer, the flow handles this gracefully:

  • The candidate is moved back to the Offered stage (this can be done programmatically via the SmartRecruiter API)
  • The recruiting stakeholder updates the CTC value
  • The stage change or field update automatically fires the webhook again
  • CPI recalculates all salary components and upserts the new values to SmartRecruiter
  • A new offer is created with the revised compensation breakdown
Key Benefit
The renegotiation cycle is entirely self-service for the recruiter — no integration team involvement is needed after the initial setup. Simply updating the CTC triggers the full recalculation automatically.
 

Conclusion

SmartRecruiter's gap in automated salary calculation for offer letters is fully addressable through a lightweight, event-driven integration pattern using SAP Integration Suite (CPI). The solution is non-invasive — it requires no customization of SmartRecruiter beyond standard field configuration and webhook setup — and it delivers a seamless recruiter experience.

Key takeaways from this implementation:

  • SmartRecruiter Webhooks provide a reliable event trigger for real-time integration
  • Candidate fields are accessible as properties via standard REST APIs
  • SAP Integration Suite (CPI) serves as the calculation engine, keeping business rules externalized and maintainable
  • The upsert API allows CPI to write back computed values directly to SmartRecruiter offer fields
  • The pattern supports renegotiation cycles without manual intervention

 

Thanks

Kunal

Product Success

SAP RIG

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 04 - Namespaces, contexts and name prefixes
Re: Cannot Continue with Migration because Microsoft JDBC Driver for SQL Server is connected
entry.content: Close all SAP Business One client sessions connected to the company database. Stop the SAP Business One Service Layer service temporarily: Open Windows Services (services.msc) Find SAP Business One Service Layer Right-click → Stop Open SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS) and connect to the database server. Open Activity Monitor: Right-click the server instance in Object Explorer → Activity Monitor Or press Ctrl+Alt+A Expand the Processes section and look for any process where: Database = your company database name Application = Microsoft JDBC Driver for SQL Server Right-click that process → Kill Process → confirm. Verify no remaining JDBC connections exist by refreshing the process list. Go back to SAP Business One and retry the migration (Migrate Account Assignments & Inventory G/L Account Determination Settings). Once the migration completes successfully, restart the SAP Business One Service Layer service.

entry.title: Existing Formation is Missing in Portfolio & Products, Systems, Formations in SAP For Me
entry.content:
Hi,

We created a Formation (with name: "SAP Business Data Cloud") in the SAP BTP Cockpit and added our BDC solutions/systems to it. This Formation is also reflected in the SAP BDC Cockpit. Everything works well. However, this Formation (of Type "Integration with SAP Business Data Cloud") is missing in "SAP For Me." 

The documentation mentions an option "Synchronize Systems" that can be selected. This option does not show for us. 

https://help.sap.com/docs/business-data-cloud/administering-sap-business-data-cloud/creating-sap-business-data-cloud-formations-5e85a38352434d199a2f6d6bc9a25221

  1. Open SAP for Me.

  2. Choose Portfolio & Products.

  3. In the My Product Packages tab, choose the Business Data Cloud product package.

  4. Open the Customer Landscape tab.

  5. Open the Systems tab.

entry.title: SAC Modeler – postingPeriod Not Mapped to Calendar Month for Company Code 2000
entry.content:
In SAP Analytics Cloud, the Balance Sheet report for Company Code 2000 is showing missing/incomplete data. Upon investigation, it has been identified that the source column "postingPeriod" is not mapped to the "Calendar Month" dimension in the Data Foundation (Facts) within the Modeler.

This mapping exists and is working correctly for Company Codes 1000 and 3000, confirming this is isolated to Company Code 2000.

entry.title: Re: How to Implement SAP Business One projects successfully
entry.content:
Great breakdown on the implementation steps. Having run 20+ SMB go-lives in the Hyderabad market, I’d add one critical nuance: the go-live week is where most SMBs lose the most money, and it’s almost never a technical issue — it’s a data readiness and real-time visibility problem.

Three failure patterns I see repeatedly:

1. Master data validation is skipped under time pressure.

Teams migrate from Tally/legacy spreadsheets and the item master is “good enough” on cutover night. Three weeks later, inventory reports are useless because unit of measure conversions weren’t standardized. The fix: run a parallel inventory count in the old system AND SAP B1 for 2 weeks before go-live. Any variance >2% stops go-live until resolved. This is where having real-time data visibility during the transition is critical — you need to see discrepancies as they happen, not in a post-mortem report.

2. User training is treated as a checkbox.

SMB owners assume the consultant will “handle it.” What actually happens: 3 PowerPoint slides, a rushed demo, and a team that can log in but can’t process a goods receipt. The result: workarounds that corrupt data, followed by 6 months of cleanup. Better model: identify 2–3 power users per department in week 1. Train them deeply. They become your go-live support army.

3. No cutover rollback plan.

If something breaks on go-live night, what’s the decision tree? Who decides to rollback vs. push through? This sounds obvious but almost no SMB implementation documents this. The result: 48 hours of chaos before the inevitable rollback — which costs more than if you’d planned for it.

The Go-Live Checklist that actually works:

  • Parallel run confirmation: Both systems running simultaneously for at least 5 business days. Cross-check every transaction — especially batch costs and inventory valuations.
  • Data freeze window: No master data changes 48 hours before cutover. Lock item masters, vendor records, and G/L mappings.
  • Rollback decision criteria: Document who decides, under what conditions, and the exact rollback procedure. Don’t improvise this at 2 AM on cutover night.
  • Go-live support team: Internal champion + consultant available for 72 hours straight. Power users in each department as first responders.
  • First-week close check: Finance runs month-end in SAP B1 within 5 days of go-live. If they can’t close the books, you have a data problem that needs immediate attention.
  • Batch cost gap analysis: For pharma and manufacturing — run a full batch cost comparison between your legacy system and SAP B1 within the first week. Any gap >1% in material costs signals a configuration issue that compounds over time.

The single best investment: A formal 3-day parallel run before go-live where the team processes every real transaction in BOTH systems simultaneously. Painful? Yes. Expensive? Yes. Cheaper than data recovery after a bad cutover? Far less.

Teams that skip this checklist average 3–4 weeks of post-go-live disruption. Teams that use it average 3–4 days. The difference is almost always data readiness and having real-time visibility into gaps before they become production problems.

What specific module are you struggling with? Happy to go deeper.

— Venkata Siva Reddy Polu, TEKROI
SAP B1 implementation for Hyderabad pharma and manufacturing SMBs

entry.title: SAP Developer News, June 4th, 2026
entry.content:
Items

ADT for VS Code available on VS Code Marketplace
- Official announcement blog post https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/abap-development-tools-for-visual-studio-code-is-now-available-on-the-vs/ba-p/14402120
- FAQ https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/abap-development-tools-for-visual-studio-code-is-now-available-on-the-vs/ba-p/14402120
- GitHub Tutorial https://github.com/SAP-samples/abap-platform-rap130/tree/main
- VS Code Marketplace Link https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=SAPSE.adt-vscode

SAP BTP ABAP Environment – release 2605
- Blog post https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-btp-abap-environment-release-2605/ba-p/14403929
- What’s New for SAP BTP ABAP environment https://help.sap.com/whats-new/7a822d3bcaa74f31b98fa315601e9c96?locale=en-US
- What’s New in Generative AI in ABAP Cloud https://help.sap.com/whats-new/4d359a7f8e8248e18f98e85ea0b9d83e?locale=en-US

June Developer Challenge details
- The challenge https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-cap-blog-posts/june-developer-challenge-cap-knowledge/ba-p/14405157

Did You Know? #19 Standardize Class Definition
- ABAP Cleaner https://github.com/SAP/abap-cleaner

Chapter titles

00:00 Intro
00:07 ADT for VS Code available on VS Code Marketplace
02:01 SAP BTP ABAP Environment – release 2605
03:20 June Developer Challenge details
04:54 Did You Know? #19 Standardize Class Definition

Transcript

[Intro]
This is the SAP Developer News for June the 4th, 2026.

[Sheena]
Hello, ABAP Developers. We have an exciting news this week. The first version of ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code extension is available in the VS Code Marketplace. This marks a significant step in modernizing the ABAP Development experience by bringing ABAP Development to one of the most widely used IDEs in the developer community. The initial release focuses on the ABAP Cloud development model and supports key artifacts such as ABAP classes, interfaces, CDS artifacts, service definitions, service binding, and behavior definitions. This extension includes essential development features such as code completion, navigation, syntax checking, debugging, ABAP unit integration, transport management, and repository search. A key highlight is that developers building RAP applications can now work on both the backend ABAP artifacts and the frontend SAP Fiori or SAPUI5 application within the same development environment. The extension also includes a built-in model context protocol or MCP server. This enables AI agents available in Visual Studio Code such as Microsoft Copilot to interact with ABAP development objects and assist developers directly within their workflow. To get started, install VS code, add the ADT extension from the marketplace, connect it to your ABAP system and you're ready to go. SAP has also published a GitHub tutorial showing how the built-in ADT MCP server can be used with AI agents to create a simple RAP application. Don't forget to also take a look at this FAQ document, which is also included in the blog post. This is just the beginning. More capabilities and ABAP development objects are planned for upcoming releases. So stay tuned.

[Shilpa]
Hello, ABAP developers. the SAP BTP ABAP environment 2605 has been released and this blog post by Nora Klemp provides an overview of the delivered features. Here are some of the key highlights. In ABAP language, Boolean expression is now supported. Yes, this was widely discussed and requested and it has now been supported. In ABAP development tools, there is a signature comparison when you are migrating from DDIC based views to CDS view entity. In ABAP restful application programming model draft is now supported in cds table entities there are two new attributes in feature control one is read-only create as well as mandatory update in analytics the WYSIWYG editor of the review booklets now supports multiple data providers in ATC a new variant is introduced to perform clean core analysis in both SAP ECC as well as SAP S/4HANA systems in landscape portal you can use Deploy Product App to deploy or update a specific product version and specific system. You can check out all the new features in What's New for SAP BTP ABAP Environment page. The new AI features is listed separately in What's New for ABAP Generative AI for ABAP Cloud. Check it out.

[DJ]
The June Developer Challenge is underway. and this month we're focusing on exercising our grey matter in order to maintain some level of cognitive discipline in this new era that's right and in the challenge we're diving into the CAP documentation to learn what's there and how to navigate to it but perhaps more importantly to learn the names for and understand the fundamentals of concepts features language constructs and more all of which make CAP what it is why because knowing what things are called and how they relate and what they do and where to find more information is the foundation of understanding and how it's built and understanding is more important than ever today. So this month there's a new question published on every working day that's 22 days in total 22 questions Each question focuses on a particular aspect or topic or area of CAP and where the answers can be found in the CAP documentation, in Capire. And also, after each day's question is done, there are links to further reading added to the end of the question post for those of you who want to learn more. It's like a five-minute mini -workout for your brain each day. What are you waiting for? See you online.

[Sheena]
Did you know that the standardized class definition feature in ABAP Cleaner helps keep class definitions consistent, improving code readability and maintainability? Usually, in ABAP class definitions, the ordering and layout of options such as public, private sections inheriting from, etc., can vary across developers and projects, leading to inconsistent code structure and reduced readability. To address this issue, ABAP Cleaner has a rule, Standardize Class Definition. It has various options like indentation, specifying number of spaces, and splitting the statement to one or multiple lines. For example, here if we select public to be in a separate line, the class definition gets rewritten with public in next line across all class definitions in your code. Similarly, for inheriting from and create statements. Now, you can see that the various class definitions within the code has the same format, thereby improving readability and maintainability and hence following SAP best practices.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA System Conversion: Reduce the memory footprint of Finance obsolete tables
entry.content:
When converting to SAP S/4HANA, Finance teams often expect an immediate reduction in database size—but this doesn’t always happen. After the System Conversion, the system still contains obsolete ECC finance data and legacy tables, which means the overall data footprint may remain unchanged at first. To take full advantage of S/4HANA’s simplified finance data model, it’s important to perform a proper post‑conversion clean up.

SAP recommends removing content from obsolete tables with notes:

SAP Note 2190137 SAP S/4HANA Finance: Reduce memory footprint of obsolete tables. SAP Note  2569435  MM-IM: Save DB memory and remove data that is not covered by Information Lifecycle Management.

Customers must not only implement the relevant SAP Notes but also complete any manual corrections or follow-up activities. These extra steps are often essential for fully realizing the desired results, including data cleanup, system optimization, and maintaining performance improvements.

Additionally, the Finance and Basis teams should identify any remaining outdated data after conversion. SAP Note 2661837 provides guidance on preventing legacy structures from occupying unnecessary database space. Once cleanup is complete, running the SAP Note 1872170 is crucial to reassess the data footprint, which supports more accurate capacity planning and cost management.

From a Finance perspective, this cleanup is a key step—not just a technical task. Reducing unnecessary data improves system performance, supports faster reporting and financial close processes, and helps control infrastructure costs. A successful S/4HANA conversion doesn’t end with go‑live; cleaning up obsolete Finance data is what unlocks the real value of the move.

Conclusion

In summary, a successful SAP S/4HANA Finance conversion involves more than just completing the technical migration and going live. While the database may not immediately shrink after the upgrade, real advantages come from cleaning up outdated Finance data and legacy tables. By following SAP’s recommended notes and collaborating with the Finance and Basis teams, organizations can eliminate unnecessary data, enhance system performance, and gain clearer insights into their actual data volume.

Please “like” if you found this blog post interesting!  

Please add in the comment section any additional topics you think are valuable to have included in this and future blog posts.

– Brought to you by the S/4HANA RIG
